item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to down-vote for a couple of different reasons. The Southern cacti aren't too interesting of an anomaly, and the forced dangerous aspect was something I thought would detract from it. The final interview saved it, though. I like the idea of these infectious creatures that may not even be human, that just want to be Southern and others to be Southern as well. The way the infection is described is actually pretty creepy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And who knew anyone would ever use that sentance?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not to mention that the Foundation might not have an anomalous object that could do that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Y'know, if you saw something like this, critter (hiveminds confuse me), on someplace like youtube, you would know you were in that part of youtube. NOT HERE! IF YOUR HEART HASN'T STOPPED FOR FIVE ADRENALINE GORGED SECONDS, I WILL SEND YOU A SIGNED CERTIFICATE STATING YOU DO NOT EXIST! SAXTON HALE, AWAAAAAAAaaaaaaayyy!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Poor Agent Myers. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: just enough for the upvote but making me want more T.T

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
CACTI FOR THE CACTUS GOD!
I like this one. The stark contrast between the hilarious first and second interviews and the unsettling feel of the third one really pleases me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole article projects a feel of "Well…shit." to me. I don't mind it at all. This is hilarious and ridiculous and awesome, so take my +1.
Whoo azzleflux!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Southern red blooded American Cacti meeting up with some folks from below the border?
Sounds like a Mexican standoff to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP. The outward wackiness of it contrasts very well with the creepiness of a hivemind of transfigured people.

One little thing about it that kind of bothers me is that I don't believe the Southwestern and Southeastern cultures of the United States to really be the same. They're similar in their accents and moral values, but you'll rarely see a Georgian in a cowboy hat. Your average Southerner has never been to a desert before, the whole cowboy lifestyle an alien thing to them. In fact, there are so many subcultures within the American Southeast and Southwest to use such umbrella terms doesn't really do them justice. From the bayous of Louisiana to the mountain hollers of North Carolina, they're just so much more to the term "Southern" than the stereotype implies. So many different subcultures and ways of life.

It hit me while writing that previous paragraph that it is precisely what makes the cacti so funny is their hilariously inaccurate, stereotypical idea of what constitutes a Southerner. Congratulations, Azzleflux. You've out-thought the Southerner who over-thinks everything. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is ridiculous.

SCP-1478 will consistently advise the subjects in desert or American southwestern topics, usually while presenting erroneous information.

I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This…This cactus…must obey…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just assumed the hive mind is calming him down, subconsciously or otherwise. "You're here now, and you're going to be here forever. But it really ain't so bad once ya git used to it, pardner!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe I have found something relevant.
Enjoy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seems to me that they weren't aware of the transmutation properties until it happened to the agent. Classifying it as Safe Euclid might not be a terrible idea.

On another note, as a resident of Texas I'm capable of doing a thick Southern-as-all-hell accent among others and am fond of the audio adaptations. Kinda tempted to submit the interview logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying the foundation should do it, I'm saying if I was destined for an eternity of being a cactus I wouldn't just be all "lol hey Jeff what's up mang?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It would be impossibly dangerous to do so.

Any attempts to remove him or revert the changes done on site would probably result in the would-be rescuers getting transformed as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if I was a foundation agent I probably would suspect that they have some SCP stored away that's specifically for changing cacti into humans. I don't know, he just seems way too resigned to an eternity of being a cactus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would you even go about turning him back? Plus, the guy is realistic enough to know that the Foundation probably wouldn't cross-test rampantly like that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, although I didn't really like the interview at the end. If I just got turned into a cactus I would be begging the foundation to somehow turn me back. Unless you wanted to get across the idea that he likes it, but it seems as if he doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my head, a D-Class really wouldn't be qualified to interview properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm quite surprised they were willing to throw another Agent at this SCP rather than a D-class after the transfiguration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was just about to say: "LET'S INTRODUCE THEM TO THE CACTI AMIGOS!"

That would probably end badly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You did it right with logs. I love when something is funny and creepy at the same time. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So… like, how nyarlathotep REALLY likes Egyptian pharaohs, Cactusthing REALLY likes Texans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love that you have to dress in desert wear and speak in a Southern accent to talk to these things.

Of course, wouldn't it be better if just no one talked to them? Then, no turning into a cactus!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After consideration, I've realized that a class change is indeed in order here. It is now Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh. I get a mild Southern-Fifthist vibe from this. That is in no way a bad thing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And you were worried it was boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to downvote this as soon as I started reading, because I'm sick to death of "wacky" SCPs whose only gimmick is talking in an unexpected way. That's not to say they're bad, I'm just really sick of them.

Then I got the transfiguration, the hive mind, and the interview with Myers, and my opinion did a 180.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is like an evil version of the Cacti Amigos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was neutral until the last log. +1.

Also, even though the cacti do technically fall under safe classification, they do have a sort of hivemind.. and now they know of the existence of Site-23, with their capabilities not fully understood yet.

Just a thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who knew Southern talking cacti could be so sinister? +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is quite good. Southern cactus hivemind… makes you wonder what caused it to happen, eh?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cool one, but it seems like it might qualify as Euclid. It seems able to convert people at will, and the instances know things they can't perceive, so it seems like it poses at least some mild danger to those who aren't -very- careful. Still, definate upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hey, Jackson, come here a second?"

"Yeah, sure, what's up?"

"You break the vending machine yesterday?"

"What? Uh, yeah, sorry bout that. Who told you that?"

"A talking cactus."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it in chat, loved it here. Creepy, well written and completely mental.

+1.
